edges.explanation: 4 million children in poverty with 1.67 million affected by the limit is the scale evidence that the policy causes harm far exceeding any work incentive
The sheer number of affected children is the primary evidence that the two-child limit is the clearest single policy failure
100 children per day into poverty from a single policy is the government's own evidence that removal is correct
DWP's own 300,000 figure makes this the most well-evidenced policy failure in the manifesto
The government's own poverty data directly justifies the removal policy
450,000 children lifted from poverty is the DWP's own estimate of the removal policy's impact
The modelled impact of removal proves the limit causes the poverty it would eliminate
800,000 increase in long-term sickness since the pandemic proves the disability rise is genuine health-driven not fraud-driven
NHS waiting lists directly driving economic inactivity proves welfare spending rises because the health system is failing
Sickness benefits rising because of NHS failure is the clearest example of welfare as a downstream node
2.8 million inactive with NHS waits as the driver is the evidence base for the joint NHS-DWP economic emergency approach
60% of children in poverty living in working households proves work alone is insufficient to prevent poverty
In-work poverty being the dominant form proves the welfare budget rises because wages are too low, not because benefits are too generous
In-work poverty demonstrates the need for taper rate reform to ensure additional earnings actually reduce poverty
3 percentage point employment effect while the majority don't respond proves the work incentive argument doesn't justify the poverty caused
£4,300/yr loss representing 10% of household income quantifies the poverty the limit imposes on affected families
25% lower earnings at age 30 shows the two-child limit creates measurable lifetime economic damage, not just temporary hardship
Lifetime earnings impact justifies direct cash transfers to children as an investment with measurable long-term returns
Unpaid carers saving £162bn/yr while having worse outcomes is the overwhelming economic and moral case for the carers' recognition package
£10-15bn/yr in unclaimed benefits is the direct evidence that automatic enrolment would reduce poverty by reaching people the current system misses
Scottish Child Payment demonstrably reducing poverty is the proven model for the England adoption policy
Scottish direct cash transfers working proves that removing the two-child limit (a form of cash transfer restoration) will reduce poverty as modelled
60%+ effective marginal tax rates mean working more barely increases net income, proving work alone cannot solve poverty without taper reform
The poverty trap from high marginal rates directly justifies UC taper rate reform
The verdict that removal is correct directly supports the claim that the limit is the clearest policy failure
£12bn welfare savings being not credible proves that cutting welfare can't fix what upstream system failures cause
Work being insufficient when 60% of poor children are in working households proves welfare spending reflects wage and housing failures
Disability rises from genuine sickness not fraud proves the welfare budget reflects NHS failure not benefit generosity
Sickness-driven disability claims are the clearest proof that welfare is downstream of NHS performance
Genuine health causes driving disability claims supports treating this as a health emergency not a welfare fraud problem
The benefit cap shifting rather than reducing poverty proves that welfare cost is driven by housing cost which the cap doesn't address
The cap failing because housing costs are the real driver proves welfare is downstream of housing policy
Removing the two-child limit depends on the claim that welfare spending reflects system failures, not generosity that should be cut
UC adequacy reform depends on the claim that current rates are inadequate because the system underpays, not because recipients don't need the money
The removal policy depends on the two-child limit being identified as the clearest policy failure
The joint NHS-DWP approach depends on the claim that welfare is downstream of NHS failure
Taper rate reform depends on the finding that work alone is insufficient because the system claws back too much
UBI being impractical at full scale but the principle being sound supports the more targeted UC adequacy commission as a pragmatic alternative
The carers' package depends on the finding that unpaid carers save £162bn while being economically penalised
Automatic enrolment depends on the £10-15bn non-take-up gap existing and being recoverable through data integration
Maintaining the two-child limit directly contradicts the evidence-based removal policy backed by the government's own data
£12bn welfare savings through sanctions contradicts the adequacy commission approach that would increase spending
Labour's two-child limit removal from April 2026 directly matches the evidence-based policy
Get Britain Working plan targeting NHS waits and mental health partially aligns with the joint NHS-DWP economic emergency approach
Lib Dems removing both the two-child limit and benefit cap exceeds the minimum evidence-based position
Independent UC adequacy commission matches the evidence-based policy almost exactly
Carer's minimum wage and expanded eligibility directly match the carers' recognition package
Removing all caps and limits goes further than the evidence-based minimum but in the right direction
Restoring benefits to pre-austerity levels aligns with adequacy reform though uses a different mechanism
£50bn spending cuts and immigration scapegoating contradicts the evidence that welfare costs are driven by system failures elsewhere
Tax cuts to incentivise employment contradicts the finding that 60% of poor children are already in working households
100 children per day into poverty from the government's own data directly contradicts the Conservative position of maintaining the limit
60% of poor children in working households proves tax cuts to incentivise work won't solve poverty driven by low wages and housing costs
Two-child limit removal within UC creates the framework for the broader Scottish Child Payment model adoption
Both policies cost billions and draw from the same welfare spending envelope, requiring prioritisation if fiscal space is limited
Treating long-term sickness as a health emergency enables taper rate reform because healthier people can benefit from better work incentives
The 3 percentage point employment effect quantifies exactly how small the work incentive is relative to 300,000 children in poverty
£4,300/yr loss per household at 10% of income sizes the poverty impact that removal would reverse
60% of poor children in working families proves £12bn savings through work conditionality cannot work when most poverty is already in-work
Lifetime earnings damage from childhood poverty supports the UBI principle that guaranteed income prevents long-term costs, even if full UBI is fiscally impractical
NHS waits driving sickness benefits shows benefit costs are caused by health system failure, not the generosity the benefit cap tries to limit
The Scottish model adoption depends on the finding that direct cash transfers to children demonstrably reduce poverty, as the two-child limit case proves

cross_domain_edges.explanation: 2.8 million economically inactive from sickness directly depends on the NHS waiting list of 7.6 million preventing treatment
Welfare spending rising because of housing costs depends on the 110,000 in temporary accommodation and housing benefit levels
Fixing welfare by fixing upstream systems requires the fiscal space that tax reform creates — the dependency chain runs through taxation
The benefit cap failing because housing costs are the real driver reinforces the housing finding that supply must increase to moderate costs
The welfare domain's joint NHS-DWP economic emergency is the same policy as the NHS domain's long-term sickness intervention
In-work poverty from low wages links directly to education and skills policy — low qualifications drive low pay
